name: reverse-proxy
context: Proxy HTTP/WebSocket traffic to backend servers with upstream groups, load balancing, and health checks
info: This skill covers using nginx as a reverse proxy: forwarding client requests to one or more backend application servers (Node.js, Python, Go, etc.), configuring upstream groups for load balancing, setting correct proxy headers, proxying WebSocket connections, and tuning timeouts and buffering. The core directive is `proxy_pass` inside a `location` block, backed by `upstream` groups for multi-server setups. Agents use this to put nginx in front of application servers — handling SSL termination at the proxy layer, distributing load with round-robin/least_conn/ip_hash, and ensuring proper client IP forwarding via X-Forwarded-For headers. See [[nginx/config]] for server block setup and [[nginx/ssl]] for HTTPS termination. See [[nginx/troubleshooting]] for 502/504 diagnosis.
triggers: put nginx in front of my app

sections.title: The proxy_pass directive
sections.body: The `proxy_pass` directive forwards requests to a backend. It takes a URL: `proxy_pass http://address:port`. When used with a URI (`proxy_pass http://backend/app/`), the matched location prefix is replaced by the URI. Without a URI, the full original request path is forwarded. In regex locations, you cannot specify a URI in proxy_pass — use `rewrite` instead. Always pair with `proxy_http_version 1.1` for keepalive support.

sections.title: Upstream groups and load balancing
sections.body: Define backend server groups with `upstream name { ... }` in the `http` context. Servers can have `weight` (default 1), `max_fails` (default 1), `fail_timeout` (default 10s), `backup`, and `down` parameters. Load balancing methods: round-robin (default), `least_conn` (fewest connections), `ip_hash` (session affinity by IP), `hash $key [consistent]` (custom key hashing), and `random two least_conn` (power of two choices). The upstream name is referenced in `proxy_pass http://name`.

sections.title: WebSocket proxying
sections.body: WebSocket requires HTTP/1.1 Upgrade mechanism. Set `proxy_http_version 1.1`, `proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade`, and `proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade"`. Increase `proxy_read_timeout` to keep connections alive (default 60s will close idle WebSockets). A typical value is `86400s` (24 hours). The hop-by-hop Connection and Upgrade headers are not forwarded by default — you must set them explicitly.

sections.title: Proxy headers for client identity
sections.body: Nginx defaults `Host` to `$proxy_host` (the upstream name) and `Connection` to `close`. Override both: set `Host $host` to forward the client's original Host header, `X-Real-IP $remote_addr` for the client IP, `X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for` for the proxy chain, and `X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme` so backends know if the request was HTTPS. Without these, backends see nginx's IP as the client and don't know about HTTPS termination.

sections.title: Response caching
sections.body: Enable caching with `proxy_cache_path` (at `http` level) and `proxy_cache zone_name` (at `location` level). Set cache validity per status code with `proxy_cache_valid`. Use `proxy_cache_key` to define the cache key (default: `$scheme$proxy_host$request_uri`). Add `X-Cache-Status` header with `$upstream_cache_status` (HIT/MISS/BYPASS/EXPIRED/STALE) for debugging. Bypass cache conditionally with `proxy_cache_bypass`. Serve stale on errors with `proxy_cache_use_stale error timeout updating http_502 http_503`.

sections.title: Failover and retry behavior
sections.body: Control how nginx retries failed requests: `proxy_next_upstream` lists conditions that trigger failover (default: `error timeout`). Add `http_502 http_503 http_504` to retry on those status codes. Limit retries with `proxy_next_upstream_tries N` and `proxy_next_upstream_timeout Ns`. A server is marked unavailable after `max_fails` failures within `fail_timeout` seconds. The `backup` parameter designates standby servers used only when all primary servers are down.
